title: Human Bioscience: Impaired Body Function Practice Test
description: A 60-question nursing practice exam for Massey University 214.201 covering ECG and shock, digestive and liver disorders, cardiovascular disorders, and endocrine, diabetes, and body mass concepts.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes a standard 12-lead ECG?
options: It uses 12 electrodes placed on the chest
It records the heart's electrical activity from 12 views using 10 electrodes
It measures blood flow through 12 cardiac chambers
It continuously monitors rhythm for 24 hours
questions.correctAnswer: It records the heart's electrical activity from 12 views using 10 electrodes

questions.prompt: Which sequence correctly follows the normal conduction pathway through the heart?
options: AV node -> SA node -> bundle branches -> Purkinje fibres
SA node -> AV node -> bundle of His -> bundle branches -> Purkinje fibres
Purkinje fibres -> bundle branches -> AV node -> SA node
SA node -> bundle branches -> atria -> AV node -> ventricles
questions.correctAnswer: SA node -> AV node -> bundle of His -> bundle branches -> Purkinje fibres

questions.prompt: If the SA node fails, which pacemaker region usually takes over at an inherent rate of about 40-60 beats per minute?
options: Atrial muscle
AV junction
Bundle branches
Purkinje network
questions.correctAnswer: AV junction

questions.prompt: On a regular ECG rhythm recorded at 25 mm/s, there are 4 large squares between consecutive R waves. What is the heart rate?
options: 60 bpm
75 bpm
100 bpm
150 bpm
questions.correctAnswer: 75 bpm

questions.prompt: Which ECG description is most consistent with atrial fibrillation?
options: Regular rhythm with saw-tooth flutter waves
Irregularly irregular rhythm with no distinct P waves
Regular wide-complex rhythm at 30 bpm
PR interval progressively lengthens until a beat is dropped
questions.correctAnswer: Irregularly irregular rhythm with no distinct P waves

questions.prompt: ST-segment elevation in contiguous leads can indicate acute myocardial injury.
questions.correctAnswer: True

questions.prompt: Ventricular fibrillation is usually a perfusing rhythm.
questions.correctAnswer: False

questions.prompt: Match each rhythm to the most appropriate ECG feature.
options: Regular rhythm below 60 bpm with normal P waves
Regular narrow-complex rhythm above 100 bpm with normal P waves
Irregularly irregular rhythm with no discrete P waves
Wide-complex rapid rhythm arising from the ventricles
PR interval longer than 0.20 seconds

pairs.answer: Regular rhythm below 60 bpm with normal P waves
Regular narrow-complex rhythm above 100 bpm with normal P waves
Irregularly irregular rhythm with no discrete P waves
Wide-complex rapid rhythm arising from the ventricles
PR interval longer than 0.20 seconds

questions.prompt: Outline a systematic approach to interpreting an ECG strip. Include how you assess rate, rhythm, P waves, PR interval, QRS complex, and ST/T changes.
questions.sampleResponseGuide: Responses should describe a stepwise method such as checking calibration, calculating rate, deciding whether the rhythm is regular, identifying P waves, measuring PR interval and QRS duration, and then reviewing ST segments and T waves for ischaemia or other abnormalities.

questions.prompt: After the first breaths at birth, which change most directly promotes functional closure of the foramen ovale?
options: Increased right atrial pressure from placental flow
Increased left atrial pressure as pulmonary blood flow rises
Reduced systemic vascular resistance from cord clamping
Immediate closure of the ductus venosus
questions.correctAnswer: Increased left atrial pressure as pulmonary blood flow rises

questions.prompt: Match each congenital cardiac defect, arising from abnormal development of the heart or great vessels, to the best description.
options: Left-to-right shunt through a ventricular septal opening
Left-to-right shunt through an atrial septal opening
Right ventricular outflow obstruction with a VSD causing cyanosis
Narrowing of the aorta causing obstructive acyanotic disease

pairs.answer: Left-to-right shunt through a ventricular septal opening
Left-to-right shunt through an atrial septal opening
Right ventricular outflow obstruction with a VSD causing cyanosis
Narrowing of the aorta causing obstructive acyanotic disease

questions.prompt: Cyanotic congenital heart defects usually involve right-to-left shunting or mixing that lowers arterial oxygen saturation.
questions.correctAnswer: True

questions.prompt: Which finding is most typical of compensated hypovolaemic shock?
options: Tachycardia with cool clammy skin
Profound bradycardia with warm flushed skin
Fixed hypotension with no compensatory response
Pulmonary oedema from acute left ventricular failure
questions.correctAnswer: Tachycardia with cool clammy skin

questions.prompt: During compensated shock, vasoconstriction and increased heart rate help maintain blood pressure and perfusion to vital organs.
questions.correctAnswer: True

questions.prompt: Define shock, name the major types, and describe the compensated, progressive, and irreversible stages with key symptoms and the body's responses in each stage.
questions.sampleResponseGuide: Responses should define shock as inadequate tissue perfusion, identify major types such as hypovolaemic, cardiogenic, distributive, and obstructive shock, and explain stage-specific responses including sympathetic activation early, worsening hypotension and cellular hypoxia in progressive shock, and refractory organ failure in irreversible shock.

questions.prompt: Which statement best defines malabsorption syndrome?
options: Excess acid secretion from the stomach
Impaired digestion or absorption of nutrients leading to nutritional deficiency
Inflammation limited to the large bowel only
Obstruction of the cystic duct by gallstones
questions.correctAnswer: Impaired digestion or absorption of nutrients leading to nutritional deficiency

questions.prompt: Pancreatic exocrine insufficiency can cause steatorrhoea and deficiency of fat-soluble vitamins.
questions.correctAnswer: True

questions.prompt: Explain how coeliac disease can cause malabsorption and outline two important nutritional consequences for a patient.
questions.sampleResponseGuide: Responses should explain that gluten-triggered immune injury causes villous atrophy in the small intestine, reducing absorptive surface area. Nutritional consequences may include iron deficiency, folate deficiency, weight loss, diarrhoea, calcium or vitamin D deficiency, and poor growth or fatigue.

questions.prompt: What is the main pathophysiological problem in gastro-oesophageal reflux disease (GERD)?
options: Lower oesophageal sphincter incompetence allowing reflux of gastric contents
Autoimmune destruction of parietal cells
Complete loss of pancreatic enzyme secretion
Portal hypertension causing oesophageal varices
questions.correctAnswer: Lower oesophageal sphincter incompetence allowing reflux of gastric contents

questions.prompt: NSAID use increases the risk of peptic ulcer disease primarily because NSAIDs:
options: Increase Helicobacter pylori growth
Inhibit prostaglandin synthesis that normally protects the gastric mucosa
Directly block bile flow from the liver
Cause autoimmune injury to the small bowel villi
questions.correctAnswer: Inhibit prostaglandin synthesis that normally protects the gastric mucosa

questions.prompt: Eradication of Helicobacter pylori reduces recurrence of many peptic ulcers.
questions.correctAnswer: True

questions.prompt: Match each digestive disorder to its hallmark feature.
options: Reflux of acidic gastric contents into the oesophagus
Inflammation of the gastric mucosa
Autodigestion from premature pancreatic enzyme activation
Luminal obstruction followed by inflammation of the appendix
Continuous mucosal inflammation beginning in the rectum

pairs.answer: Reflux of acidic gastric contents into the oesophagus
Inflammation of the gastric mucosa
Autodigestion from premature pancreatic enzyme activation
Luminal obstruction followed by inflammation of the appendix
Continuous mucosal inflammation beginning in the rectum

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains the pathophysiology of acute pancreatitis?
options: Bile salts permanently replace pancreatic tissue
Digestive enzymes activate prematurely within the pancreas and damage tissue
The pancreas stops releasing insulin after viral infection
The appendix ruptures and inflames the pancreas
questions.correctAnswer: Digestive enzymes activate prematurely within the pancreas and damage tissue

questions.prompt: Appendicitis commonly begins when the appendiceal lumen is obstructed by:
options: Excess bile entering the colon
A faecalith or lymphoid hyperplasia
Loss of gastric acid secretion
Autoimmune destruction of colonic smooth muscle
questions.correctAnswer: A faecalith or lymphoid hyperplasia

questions.prompt: Crohn disease may affect any part of the gastrointestinal tract, involves transmural inflammation, and may lead to complications such as fistulae or strictures.
questions.correctAnswer: True

questions.prompt: Match each lower gastrointestinal condition to the best description.
options: Outpouchings in the colon that may be asymptomatic
Inflamed or infected diverticula causing localized pain and fever
Benign mucosal growth that may become dysplastic
Malignant neoplasm often associated with altered bowel habit or bleeding
Mechanical blockage preventing passage of intestinal contents

pairs.answer: Outpouchings in the colon that may be asymptomatic
Inflamed or infected diverticula causing localized pain and fever
Benign mucosal growth that may become dysplastic
Malignant neoplasm often associated with altered bowel habit or bleeding
Mechanical blockage preventing passage of intestinal contents

questions.prompt: Describe two common causes of intestinal obstruction and explain the main pathophysiological consequences, how it is commonly diagnosed, and one serious complication.
questions.sampleResponseGuide: Responses should mention causes such as adhesions, hernia, tumour, volvulus, or impacted faeces, then explain fluid sequestration, vomiting, distension, electrolyte imbalance, reduced perfusion, and diagnosis using clinical assessment plus imaging such as abdominal X-ray or CT. Serious complications may include bowel ischaemia, perforation, sepsis, or peritonitis.

questions.prompt: Explain how bilirubin is used in assessing jaundice and describe what patterns may suggest pre-hepatic, hepatic, or post-hepatic disease.
questions.sampleResponseGuide: Responses should explain that bilirubin results help identify the cause of jaundice, with unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia suggesting increased production or impaired conjugation, mixed or hepatocellular patterns occurring with liver injury, and conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia with dark urine and pale stools suggesting cholestasis or biliary obstruction.

questions.prompt: Match each liver-related test or substance to its main clinical relevance.
options: Hepatocellular injury marker
Cholestasis or biliary obstruction marker
Marker of hepatic synthetic function
Marker of blood clotting factor synthesis
Pigment helpful in assessing jaundice

pairs.answer: Hepatocellular injury marker
Cholestasis or biliary obstruction marker
Marker of hepatic synthetic function
Marker of blood clotting factor synthesis
Pigment helpful in assessing jaundice

questions.prompt: The liver receives blood from both the hepatic artery and the portal vein, performs functions such as bile production and synthesis of albumin and clotting factors, and cirrhosis can distort this architecture and contribute to portal hypertension.
questions.correctAnswer: True

questions.prompt: Which statement best defines hypertension and differentiates primary and secondary hypertension?
options: Primary hypertension is caused by heart failure, whereas secondary hypertension is caused by angina

Hypertension is persistently elevated arterial blood pressure; primary hypertension has no single identifiable cause, whereas secondary hypertension results from an underlying condition such as renal or endocrine disease
Primary hypertension occurs only in older adults, whereas secondary hypertension occurs only in children
Primary hypertension is always symptomatic, whereas secondary hypertension is always asymptomatic
questions.correctAnswer: Hypertension is persistently elevated arterial blood pressure; primary hypertension has no single identifiable cause, whereas secondary hypertension results from an underlying condition such as renal or endocrine disease

questions.prompt: Cardiovascular disease remains a leading cause of death and illness in New Zealand and contributes to important health inequities.
questions.correctAnswer: True

questions.prompt: Match each cardiovascular medication class to its main physiological effect.
options: Reduces formation of angiotensin II
Slows heart rate and reduces renin release
Promotes sodium and water excretion
Relaxes vascular smooth muscle by limiting calcium entry
Nitric oxide-mediated venous and coronary dilation

pairs.answer: Reduces formation of angiotensin II
Slows heart rate and reduces renin release
Promotes sodium and water excretion
Relaxes vascular smooth muscle by limiting calcium entry
Nitric oxide-mediated venous and coronary dilation

questions.prompt: Explain how long-standing hypertension can damage target organs. Include at least three outcomes affecting the heart, brain, kidneys, or blood vessels.
questions.sampleResponseGuide: Responses should explain persistent pressure load and endothelial injury leading to problems such as left ventricular hypertrophy, heart failure, coronary disease, stroke, chronic kidney disease, retinopathy, and accelerated atherosclerosis.

questions.prompt: Which event is an early step in the development of atherosclerosis?
options: Immediate calcification of the arterial media
Endothelial injury or dysfunction with lipid entry into the vessel wall
Complete coronary artery thrombosis in a healthy artery
Destruction of cardiac valves by infection
questions.correctAnswer: Endothelial injury or dysfunction with lipid entry into the vessel wall

questions.prompt: Stable angina usually occurs with exertion and often improves with rest or glyceryl trinitrate.
questions.correctAnswer: True

questions.prompt: Which statement best distinguishes myocardial infarction from angina?
options: Angina always causes permanent myocardial necrosis, whereas MI does not
MI involves prolonged ischaemia causing myocardial necrosis and raised cardiac biomarkers
Angina always produces ST elevation and MI never does
MI pain is always relieved immediately by rest
questions.correctAnswer: MI involves prolonged ischaemia causing myocardial necrosis and raised cardiac biomarkers

questions.prompt: Match each cardiovascular condition to the best description.
options: Transient myocardial ischaemia without necrosis
Myocardial necrosis from prolonged coronary occlusion
Damage caused by long-term elevated blood pressure
Sudden focal neurological deficit from cerebral ischaemia or haemorrhage
Temporary focal neurological deficit that fully resolves

pairs.answer: Transient myocardial ischaemia without necrosis
Myocardial necrosis from prolonged coronary occlusion
Damage caused by long-term elevated blood pressure
Sudden focal neurological deficit from cerebral ischaemia or haemorrhage
Temporary focal neurological deficit that fully resolves

questions.prompt: A common cause of right-sided heart failure is:
options: Untreated appendicitis
Long-standing left-sided heart failure or pulmonary hypertension
Acute pancreatitis
Hyperthyroidism alone
questions.correctAnswer: Long-standing left-sided heart failure or pulmonary hypertension

questions.prompt: Right-sided heart failure commonly causes peripheral oedema, hepatomegaly, and raised jugular venous pressure.
questions.correctAnswer: True

questions.prompt: Which statement best distinguishes HFrEF from HFpEF?
options: HFrEF has reduced ejection fraction from impaired contractility, whereas HFpEF has preserved ejection fraction but impaired ventricular relaxation and filling
HFrEF affects only the right ventricle, whereas HFpEF affects only the left ventricle
HFpEF always results from myocardial infarction, whereas HFrEF never does
There is no difference in ventricular pumping or filling between them
questions.correctAnswer: HFrEF has reduced ejection fraction from impaired contractility, whereas HFpEF has preserved ejection fraction but impaired ventricular relaxation and filling

questions.prompt: Compare treatment priorities for stable angina, acute myocardial infarction, and chronic heart failure.
questions.sampleResponseGuide: Responses should contrast symptom relief and risk reduction for angina, urgent reperfusion and antiplatelet or anticoagulant therapy for acute MI, and long-term management of heart failure with medicines such as ACE inhibitors, beta blockers, diuretics, plus fluid, salt, and lifestyle management as appropriate.

questions.prompt: Which statement about lipoproteins is correct?
options: HDL is the most atherogenic lipoprotein because it deposits cholesterol into arterial walls
LDL transports cholesterol to tissues and, when elevated, contributes to atherosclerosis
VLDL removes cholesterol from plaques and strongly protects against CVD
Hyperlipidaemia means abnormally low levels of plasma lipids
questions.correctAnswer: LDL transports cholesterol to tissues and, when elevated, contributes to atherosclerosis

questions.prompt: Cardiac troponin is a plasma biomarker used to support diagnosis of myocardial infarction because it rises with myocardial cell injury.
questions.correctAnswer: True

questions.prompt: Which combination best reflects evidence-based health promotion to reduce cardiovascular risk?
options: Smoking cessation, regular physical activity, blood pressure and lipid control, and a diet lower in saturated fat and salt
Smoking only on weekends, avoiding fruit, and increasing salt intake
Stopping all exercise to reduce cardiac workload permanently
Using antibiotics whenever chest pain occurs
questions.correctAnswer: Smoking cessation, regular physical activity, blood pressure and lipid control, and a diet lower in saturated fat and salt

questions.prompt: What is the main function of the endocrine system?
options: To carry oxygen directly to tissues
To secrete hormones into the bloodstream to regulate body processes
To digest nutrients within the intestinal lumen
To produce antibodies against all pathogens
questions.correctAnswer: To secrete hormones into the bloodstream to regulate body processes

questions.prompt: Negative feedback is a common mechanism that controls hormone production and secretion.
questions.correctAnswer: True

questions.prompt: Match each hormone to its main source.
options: Pancreatic beta cells
Adrenal cortex zona fasciculata
Thyroid follicular cells
Adrenal cortex zona glomerulosa
Parathyroid chief cells

pairs.answer: Pancreatic beta cells
Adrenal cortex zona fasciculata
Thyroid follicular cells
Adrenal cortex zona glomerulosa
Parathyroid chief cells

questions.prompt: What is the main principle behind endocrine stimulation and suppression tests?
options: They measure only structural abnormalities on X-ray
They assess whether a gland or feedback axis appropriately increases or decreases hormone secretion after a controlled challenge
They permanently replace a missing hormone
They are used only to diagnose diabetes
questions.correctAnswer: They assess whether a gland or feedback axis appropriately increases or decreases hormone secretion after a controlled challenge

questions.prompt: Which finding is most consistent with Cushing syndrome due to excess cortisol?
options: Weight loss, hypotension, and hyperpigmentation from cortisol deficiency
Central weight gain, proximal muscle weakness, and thin fragile skin
Bradycardia, cold intolerance, and constipation only
Tetany caused by severe hypocalcaemia
questions.correctAnswer: Central weight gain, proximal muscle weakness, and thin fragile skin

questions.prompt: Match each thyroid disorder or finding to the best description.
options: Autoimmune hyperthyroidism often with diffuse goitre and eye signs
Autoimmune hypothyroidism often associated with anti-TPO antibodies
Raised TSH with low free T4
Low TSH with high free T4
Levothyroxine replacement

pairs.answer: Autoimmune hyperthyroidism often with diffuse goitre and eye signs
Autoimmune hypothyroidism often associated with anti-TPO antibodies
Raised TSH with low free T4
Low TSH with high free T4
Levothyroxine replacement

questions.prompt: Primary hypothyroidism usually produces a raised TSH and a low free T4.
questions.correctAnswer: True

questions.prompt: Explain how absolute insulin deficiency in type 1 diabetes and relative insulin deficiency with insulin resistance in type 2 diabetes produce hyperglycaemia, polyuria, polydipsia, and common acute complications.
questions.sampleResponseGuide: Responses should explain reduced glucose uptake and increased hepatic glucose output causing hyperglycaemia, osmotic diuresis causing polyuria and polydipsia, and how marked insulin deficiency in type 1 diabetes promotes lipolysis, ketogenesis, and diabetic ketoacidosis, while severe hyperglycaemia in type 2 diabetes may lead to hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state.

questions.prompt: Which comparison between type 1 diabetes mellitus (T1DM) and type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) is most accurate?
options: T1DM is always caused by obesity, whereas T2DM is always autoimmune

T1DM often has rapid onset with autoimmune beta-cell destruction and absolute insulin deficiency, whereas T2DM usually develops more gradually and is strongly associated with insulin resistance, excess weight, and family history
T1DM never causes ketoacidosis, whereas T2DM always does
T2DM occurs only in older adults and cannot occur in younger people
questions.correctAnswer: T1DM often has rapid onset with autoimmune beta-cell destruction and absolute insulin deficiency, whereas T2DM usually develops more gradually and is strongly associated with insulin resistance, excess weight, and family history

questions.prompt: Which test best reflects average blood glucose control over the previous 2-3 months and is commonly used in screening, diagnosis, and monitoring of diabetes?
options: Random urine ketones
HbA1c
Serum sodium
Capillary glucose at a single time point
questions.correctAnswer: HbA1c

questions.prompt: Diabetic ketoacidosis is more common in type 1 diabetes, whereas hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state is more typical of type 2 diabetes.
questions.correctAnswer: True

questions.prompt: Match each metabolic syndrome component to the abnormal finding.
options: Raised waist circumference
Raised triglycerides
Reduced HDL cholesterol
Raised blood pressure
Raised fasting glucose

pairs.answer: Raised waist circumference
Raised triglycerides
Reduced HDL cholesterol
Raised blood pressure
Raised fasting glucose

questions.prompt: Compare key management priorities for type 1 and type 2 diabetes, including pharmacological treatment, monitoring, education, lifestyle measures, and prevention of chronic complications.
questions.sampleResponseGuide: Responses should note that type 1 diabetes requires insulin replacement and regular glucose monitoring, while type 2 diabetes management often begins with lifestyle change and may include oral agents and later insulin. Both require education, monitoring for complications, nutrition and activity support, and risk-factor reduction to prevent chronic microvascular and macrovascular complications.

questions.prompt: Describe where visceral fat is located and explain why it carries greater health risk than peripheral fat.
questions.sampleResponseGuide: Responses should state that visceral fat is stored within the abdominal cavity around internal organs and is associated with greater inflammatory activity, insulin resistance, dyslipidaemia, and higher risk of metabolic syndrome, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease.

questions.prompt: Which combination lists modifiable factors that commonly increase obesity and cardiometabolic risk, in contrast to non-modifiable factors such as age, genetics, and ethnicity?
options: Energy-dense diet, physical inactivity, and excess sugary drink intake
Ageing, ethnicity, and family history only
Height, sex assigned at birth, and eye colour
Appendicitis, coeliac disease, and iron deficiency
questions.correctAnswer: Energy-dense diet, physical inactivity, and excess sugary drink intake
